title: Quick check: Custom claims and authz

questions.prompt: Roles in JWT claims vs roles in a database table — when to use which?

choices.text: JWT claims (via Custom Access Token Hook): fast (no DB lookup per query), but changes require token refresh — suitable for slowly-changing global roles (admin, user, plan tier). DB table: changes take effect immediately, supports per-resource fine-grained permissions, but adds a join per query — suitable for granular permissions, team-scoped roles, dynamic permission changes. Many apps use both: global role in JWT for fast access; per-resource permissions in tables.

questions.explanation: JWT for slow-changing fast-read roles; DB for dynamic granular permissions.

questions.prompt: Multi-tenant SaaS where each user can belong to multiple orgs and switch between them. How to scope queries?

choices.text: Membership table linking users to tenants with a role column. JWT carries a `current_tenant_id` claim set when the user picks an org from a dropdown — your server function re-issues the JWT with the new claim. RLS policies check `tenant_id = current_tenant_id()` AND `EXISTS in tenant_members` so users only see the currently-selected tenant's data AND only tenants they're a member of. Tenant switching = token refresh; policies enforce isolation; admins can do everything inside their tenant via role checks in tenant_members.

questions.explanation: Memberships table + current_tenant_id claim + policies checking both tenant match and membership.
